---
title: "What Do East and nQuery Compute *Exactly* ?"
subtitle: "*A short empirical exploration of sample‑size exact engines for two‑sample binomials*"
image: "man/figures/exact.png"
bibliography: references.bibtex
author:
  - name: Arocena Thomas
    orcid: 0009-0002-7014-220X
    email: arocena.thomas@gmail.com
    role: "Stagiaire"
    affiliations: Oncostat
  - name: Dan Chaltiel
    email: dan.chaltiel@gustaveroussy.fr
    orcid: 0000-0003-3488-779X
    role: "Encadrant"
  - name: Anne Lourdessamy
    email: anne.lourdessamy@gustaveroussy.fr
    orcid: 0009-0006-9051-3302
    role: "Encadrante"
date: last-modified
format: html
lightbox:
  match: auto
  effect: fade
  desc-position: right
---
```{r}
#| label: setup
library(ggplot2)
library(gt)
source("R/theming_flatly.R")
source("R/theming_darkly.R")
```

```{r}
x <- readr::read_rds("outputs/explore_bin2exact.rds")
```


Sample‑size software often labels a design simply as “exact,” but different exact tests can lead to noticeably different sample sizes. We explored what **nQuery** and **East** are *exactly* doing by comparing their outputs to transparent reference implementations from the [**bbssr**](https://github.com/gosukehommaEX/bbssr) R package.

## What we compared

To understand how nQuery and East behave, we compared their sample‑size outputs with several well‑established exact and asymptotic tests implemented transparently in the [**bbssr**](https://github.com/gosukehommaEX/bbssr) R package. Specifically, we examined:

*   **Pearson Chi‑squared Test**
*   **Fisher Exact Test**
*   **Fisher Mid‑p Test**
*   **Z‑pooled Exact Unconditional Test**
*   **Boschloo Exact Unconditional Test**

These methods cover both **conditional** (Fisher) and **unconditional** families, as well as an asymptotic benchmark (chi‑squared). Short, clear explanations of each test are available in Gosuke Homma’s documentation: [*Statistical methods explained*](https://htmlpreview.github.io/?https://github.com/gosukehommaEX/bbssr/blob/main/doc/bbssr-statistical-methods.html)

## How did we compare

*   **nQuery** states explicitly that its two‑proportion exact design uses **Fisher’s exact test**.
*   **East**, as documented in its User Manual, uses an **unconditional exact superiority test** based on maximizing the probability of rejecting the null over all nuisance parameters (a score‑type unconditional approach, not Fisher’s conditional test).

To see whether the software behaves as expected, we computed an **N‑ratio** comparing each exact method to the sample size returned by the software.

$$
\text{N‑ratio} = \frac{N_{\text{method}}}{N_{\text{software}}}.
$$

If the software truly corresponds to a given test, that test’s N‑ratio should cluster around 1.

## Comparison results

### nQuery: pattern matches Fisher's exact

::: {.light-content}
```{r}
#| out-width: 100%
gt_theme_flatly(x$tables$gt_n_ratio_nquery)
x$plots$p_n_ratio_nquery + theme_flatly()
```
:::

::: {.dark-content}
```{r}
#| out-width: 100%
gt_theme_darkly(x$tables$gt_n_ratio_nquery)
x$plots$p_n_ratio_nquery + theme_darkly()
```
:::


In the nQuery plot, Fisher’s N‑ratio sits tightly around 1 across all scenarios.  
Unconditional tests (Boschloo, mid‑p, Z‑pooled) systematically fall below 1, as expected given their higher power.

### East: behaves like an unconditional exact test

East’s documentation makes it *clear*^[East 6 User Manual, Appendix U: Theory-Design - Binomial Two-Sample Exact Tests, U.2 Power of Unconditional Test of Superiority, p3314] that the method is **unconditional**. The empirical results confirm this:

::: {.light-content}
```{r}
#| out-width: 100%
gt_theme_flatly(x$tables$gt_n_ratio_east)
x$plots$p_n_ratio_east + theme_flatly()
```
:::

::: {.dark-content}
```{r}
#| out-width: 100%
gt_theme_darkly(x$tables$gt_n_ratio_east)
x$plots$p_n_ratio_east + theme_darkly()
```
:::

*   Unconditional method **Z‑pooled** lies very close to N‑ratio = 1.
*   Fisher’s test tends to produce values *above* 1 because Fisher is more conservative, requiring slightly larger sample sizes.

This is the signature of an **unconditional exact** design.
